data.title: Best things to do in Nara, Japanese city that’s more than just an Osaka or Kyoto side trip
data.description:
Conveniently located in the centre of western Japan’s Kansai region, Nara has long been a popular day-trip destination for tourists staying in Osaka and Kyoto, which are both less than an hour away via public transport.
Nara was Japan’s first permanent capital, from AD710 to AD794, and is famous for being home to some of the oldest temples and shrines in the country, as well as a population of tame, free-roaming deer that graze around them.
The Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara is a Unesco World Heritage site featuring complexes in and around Nara Park, just outside the city centre. The most famous of these is the majestic Todaiji Temple, which houses one of the world’s largest bronze Buddha statues.
Nara preserves the serene atmosphere of early Japanese Buddhism and Shinto, Japan’s indigenous faith. This is seen at the Kasugataisha Shrine, with its iconic lantern-lined paths, and the Horyuji Temple complex, home to the world’s oldest surviving wooden structure.

Widely regarded as the birthplace of Japanese culture, Nara offers an awe-inspiring window back to the beginnings of Japanese civilisation, all in walkable, green surroundings. The myriad experiences it offers make it much more than just a pit stop.
“People know Nara, but many still see it as a place to stop by for a few hours on the way to somewhere else,” says Klaus Christandl, general manager of JW Marriott Hotel Nara.
To him, the city offers the quintessential experience of Japan.
“Nara holds many of the qualities that people imagine when they think about Japan at its most authentic. There is a strong sense of history, but it never feels distant or staged.”

The city offers a wide range of attractions for anyone who wants to get a genuine taste of Japanese traditions away from the more intense crowds in neighbouring Osaka and over-touristed Kyoto.
Here are several reasons to extend your stay in the historical city.
Ryokans
When one thinks of ryokans – traditional Japanese inns – Kyoto, or Hakone in eastern Japan, are typically among the first places that come to mind. But Nara also has ryokans that offer experiences different from those in other cities.
Nara’s ryokans are often smaller, family-run establishments – many have been in the same families for generations – located near Nara Park or in serene, rural settings on the city’s outskirts.
The feel is less about lavish luxury and more about authenticity and hospitality – or “omotenashi” in Japanese. They typically feature a more intimate, historically grounded and accessible stay.
One thing to note is that there are no natural hot springs in Nara’s city centre. A few ryokans on the outskirts of town or in the nearby mountain town of Yoshino may have access to natural hot spring water, but it is not a standard feature.
Nakatanidou
For a bit of edible history, head to Nakatanidou (29 Hashimotocho, Nara, 630-8217), a mochi (Japanese rice cake) shop in central Nara.
Mochi pounding (mochitsuki) is a centuries-old Japanese ritual with religious roots. Originating as a sacred harvest offering to the gods, it evolved into a communal New Year’s tradition.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by 中谷堂【公式】Nakatanidou (@nakatanidou.jp)



The rhythmic pounding symbolises unity and good fortune, connecting modern celebrations to ancient agricultural and spiritual practices.
Nakatanidou is known for the speed at which it pounds its rice dough, which is eventually rolled up into tasty snacks. The store was Japan’s high-speed mochitsuki champion in 2005 and 2006.
Tea Uekubo
Junichi Uekubo, the current manager of this tea farm located on the outskirts of Nara (4309-6 Tsukigasemomogano, Nara, 630-2306), is the seventh generation of his family to tend its lands.
The brand is famous for its hand-rolled green tea, or temomi-cha, made by Uekubo himself. His traditional methods include using natural fertilisers such as seafood shells to imbue the tea with a deep umami flavour. He produces only 400 grams of the tea annually.

Uekubo was named Japan’s Grand Tea Master in 2017 and has won major awards for this unique, flavourful tea.
Unfortunately, the hand-rolled tea is not available to buy, but visitors can book private tasting sessions where they can also learn about the production process and Japanese tea culture.
Bar scene
For those who love to discover creative cocktails, Lamp Bar (26 Tsunofuricho, Nara, 630-8224) is the heart of Nara’s burgeoning bar scene.
The sole bartender of the 12-seat bar, which is listed at No 46 on Asia’s 50 Best Bars 2025 list, is Michito Kaneko. There is no menu, and Kaneko is renowned for making bespoke cocktails tailored to guests’ individual tastes.
Another popular Nara bar is Savant (30-3 Tsubaicho, Nara, 630-8343), which is housed in a refurbished 100-year-old town house. There is no signage, making this a true hidden gem.
The Flying Stag on the ground floor of the JW Marriott Hotel Nara (1 Chome-1-1 Sanjooji, Nara, 630-8013) is an easy option with an extensive whisky list as well as seasonal cocktails.

Kakigori
Nara is home to the Himuro Shrine, a place of worship dedicated to an ice deity. So it is little wonder that kakigori (Japanese shaved-ice dessert) holds a special place in the hearts of locals in the area.
Kakigori has ancient roots in Japan, with records of shaved ice served to emperors in Nara over a thousand years ago.
Nara is home to a lot of artisanal kakigori shops these days, but Houseki-bako (47 Mochiidonocho, Nara, 630-8222) was a major catalyst for Nara’s kakigori boom. The shop is famous for its light, fluffy, multilayered product using pure, slow-frozen ice and local ingredients such as Yamato matcha and fresh milk.
Counter seating there is limited, so reservations are required.
Mount Yoshino

In spring, a breathtaking panorama unfolds as thousands of cherry trees bloom in waves across Mount Yoshino’s slopes. Come autumn, those same slopes are covered in vibrant crimson and gold maples.
To reach Yoshino from Nara, take the train to Yoshino Station, and from there take a scenic ride on a gondola car up to the town.
The area is famously crowded during peak seasons, but the stunning vistas and historic atmosphere, with temples nestled among the foliage, make the journey worthwhile.

data.title: China’s resolve to invest in people is economic good sense
data.description:
One thing China has in abundance is people. An economic strategy to invest more in them, including in education, therefore has important implications. The ultimate goal is to stimulate their spending and generate more domestic economic activity to reduce reliance on exports.
The idea gained momentum among proposals for the 15th five-year plan beginning next year, which will be considered by the “two sessions” of the country’s top legislature and political advisory body early next year. It was reaffirmed at this month’s agenda-setting central economic work conference, at which leaders said the country must “combine investment in physical assets with investment in people”.
Government plays a central role in China’s economic investment. That has amounted mainly to investment in hardware or infrastructure such as bridges, railways and airports. The idea is that more should go into education, public healthcare, social welfare and measures to lift low-income households.
In the latest evidence of weak domestic consumption, retail sales growth slowed for the sixth consecutive month in November, with retail sales rising just 1.3 per cent year on year after growing 2.9 per cent in October, according to the National Bureau of Statistics.
The reasons include lack of consumer confidence in spending. Among the middle class, for example, wealth is tied to an ailing property market, and priorities still include saving for children’s education or elderly care.
A book introducing the five-year plan proposals, produced by a Communist Party-run publishing house, says: “The return from investing in physical assets is declining while we have long underinvested in livelihoods and comprehensive human development. In the transformation of growth towards a demand- and innovation-driven pattern, it is imperative to increase investment in people.”
This means investing more in opportunities for people rather than the use of direct subsidies. Given that education is a priority of Chinese families, China can be expected to spend a lot more on it.
This dovetails with competition with the United States, in which a revamp of the education system is key. This sector looks likely to benefit the most in the coming years.
Hong Kong has a supporting role to play as a superconnector to the mainland that is also an education hub. In that regard, Secretary for Education Christine Choi Yuk-lin has announced that a “Study in Hong Kong” week will be held in February to highlight the city’s credentials.

data.title: China’s AgiBot targets US$142 million revenue as march of humanoid robots gathers pace
data.description:
AgiBot, one of the fastest-growing robotics start-ups in China, has set its sights on exceeding 1 billion yuan (US$142 million) in revenue for 2025, as the industry accelerates the commercialisation and large-scale deployment of humanoid robots.
AgiBot was expected to ship a total of 5,000 humanoid robots in 2025, chairman and CEO Deng Taihua said in a company event on Monday.
AgiBot’s output and sales targets, which the start-up confirmed on Tuesday, were expected to maintain “multi-fold” growth in 2026, according to Deng, with the company pushing ahead with commercialisation.
As part of its commercial strategy, AgiBot launched BotShare, a platform designed to make robot rental as convenient as power bank sharing, according to a company announcement on Monday.

The rental platform, targeted to expand to more than 200 mainland Chinese cities by 2026, would bring together users, developers, rental service providers and manufacturers to make humanoid robots more accessible to the public, said Jiang Qingsong, a partner at AgiBot and chairman of Botshare.
The industry is rapidly pivoting towards commercialisation amid fierce competition among a new wave of robot makers racing for dominance under Beijing’s support for the hi-tech sector.
The Chinese government views “embodied intelligence” – the integration of artificial intelligence into hardware such as humanoid robots – as a strategic industry to transform the country’s manufacturing sector and drive future economic growth.
Galbot, a Beijing-based robotics start-up backed by the Hong Kong government, recently secured an order for 1,000 robots from Baida Precision Manufacturing, a Chinese compressor component maker.
The deal came days after Galbot raised US$300 million from investors including state-owned telecommunications giant China Mobile, marking the largest funding round to date for the embodied intelligence industry, the company said.
The robots would be integrated into Baida’s workflow, from raw material warehousing and precision machining to quality inspection, according to Galbot.
Chinese firms are racing to find practical applications for their robots in various manufacturing settings.
In August, AgiBot secured an order from car parts manufacturer Fulin Precision to deploy nearly 100 robots across its factories. In July, the company and Hangzhou-based Unitree Robotics won contracts worth 124 million yuan from China Mobile.
Earlier this month, AgiBot’s 5,000th mass-produced humanoid robot rolled off the line at its factory in Shanghai, just two years after the company’s establishment.

data.title: The private members’ clubs offering luxury accommodation, from Soho House to Saint James
data.description:
Ultra-high-net-worth individuals are becoming especially discerning when it comes to choosing hotels. While top-notch amenities, luxe accommodation and customised services are now a given, a sense of seclusion and exclusivity is becoming more important.
Hoteliers have risen to the challenge by experimenting with strategies ranging from launching new “exclusive” spaces and brands, to simply increasing rates, but many properties still struggle to deliver when it comes to important intangibles like privacy and personalisation.

Rising to the occasion is the private members’ club, a concept that dates back to the gentleman’s clubs of the 17th century. Built around values such as discretion and like-mindedness, these clubs initially started out as sanctuaries where members could network and socialise while enjoying a range of amenities ranging from entertainment and wellness, to food and drinks. Over time these places thankfully became somewhat more egalitarian and recent years have seen a fresh wave of innovation.
The premise changed when British group Soho House opened its first club in London in 1995 with an additional perk: accommodation. The members’ club hotel was born.

Today other clubs are rooms that rival any luxury hotel. Popular names include Saint James Club in Paris; The Arts Club, The Twenty Two and Home House in London; The Aster and San Vicente Bungalows in Los Angeles (the latter is a favourite of celebrities like Demi Moore); and Aethos in Milan. Even the wellness and hospitality industries have got in on the act with US fitness chain Equinox and Italian restaurant Cipriani adding accommodation for those at the most elevated tier of membership.

So why do these clubs hit the right notes for certain travellers?
“True hospitality extends beyond facilities and service – it’s about cultivating a sense of conviviality, creating environments where culture and connection thrive. The members’ club model allows hoteliers to know their guests before they arrive, to curate an intentional audience, and to ensure alignment with the atmosphere and experiences being offered,” says Luca Del Bono, founder of Onda, an invitation-only platform that gives access to over 250 private members’ clubs globally.

Another key draw is the element of privacy and discretion – something not always guaranteed with a five-star hotel. Hotels are open to the public, while the raison d’être of the private club is to be extremely selective, with access restricted to members and perhaps, select guests. By nature, these clubs tend to be smaller, and any necessary mingling tends to be more meaningful as you’re sharing a space with a like-minded community.
“This allows for more intimate programming, from cultural gatherings to wellness experiences, designed for members. It’s a lifestyle offering that goes far beyond the transactional nature of a traditional hotel stay,” explains Del Bono.

One such lifestyle offering is Carlyle & Co, a private members’ club inspired by New York’s legendary The Carlyle hotel that was launched in 2021, with its first outpost run by Hong Kong’s Rosewood Hotel Group.
“The hotel and club have a deep relationship. The Carlyle was always seen as a type of private members’ club with a celebrated mystique and eclectic community. Carlyle & Co draws on this spirit and takes it further with unrivalled entertainment, a music programme and more. The club environment is different to that of a hotel, and we want it to be different,” says Jonathan Frolich, vice-president of Carlyle & Co Global.

The members’ experience at the club reflects this. Guests can stay in one of only eight bedrooms, all designed to resemble a New York pied-à-terre. Each is named after a famous guest who frequented the famous hotel and features custom details including bedlinen, art and objects that tell something of each former guest’s story.
The programming is another perk with an exclusive line-up of jazz singers at Café Carlyle, and a range of talks and other initiatives for guests. Then you have the service, which tends to be more personalised when compared to a hotel.

“Our service style is more relaxed, with a focus on personality and passion through members of the team, rather than [relying on] scripts. There needs to be a balance of warmth in addition to the excellence factor, precision and attention to detail. This is hospitality, but not in the traditional sense,” says Frolich.
“The great advantage of a private club is its homely feel. From the moment you arrive, you’re not just a guest – you’re part of something. The experience is personal and respectful. Luxury hotels may recall your favourite table or pillow preference, but the relationship is often less attentive,” adds Del Bono.

The lure of the private members’ clubs has also proliferated into new business models for the hotel industry. The newly opened Mett Singapore, for example, features a private club called Madison House; while celebrity chef José Andrés has announced the launch of The Bazaar House, a new hotel with a private members’ club in Washington DC. Hotel Group IHG also plans to launch Six Senses Place, a new brand of private members’ clubs designed to be social and wellness hubs.
“Increasingly, we’re seeing a convergence of clubs with private suites, or hotels with discreet, members-only entrances offering the rare combination of privacy, intimacy and belonging. I believe we’ll see the club model evolve into residences, retreats and integrated lifestyle destinations. The appeal of living, even temporarily, within a curated community is powerful,” says Del Bono.

As the number of private members’ clubs with rooms grows, experts believe they will only add to the options for luxury travellers.
“Members’ clubs and hotels complement each other. Hotels can provide an amazing sense of discovery and connection to a locale, while a members’ club offers an element of exclusivity and scarcity that gives the person staying in the bedroom access to a private world,” says Frolich.

data.title: ‘60 Minutes’ story on Trump immigration crackdown accidentally airs on Canadian app
data.description:
A news segment about the Trump administration’s immigration policy that was abruptly pulled from “60 Minutes” was mistakenly aired on a television app after the last-minute decision not to air it touched off a public debate about journalistic independence.
The segment featured interviews with migrants who were sent to a notorious El Salvador prison called the Terrorism Confinement Centre, or CECOT, under US President Donald Trump’s aggressive crackdown on immigration.
The story was pulled from Global Television Network, one of Canada’s largest networks, but still ran on the network’s app.
Global Television Network swiftly corrected the error, but copies of it continued to float around the internet and pop up before being taken down.
“Paramount’s content protection team is in the process of routine take-down orders for the unaired and unauthorised segment,” a CBS spokesperson said on Tuesday via email.

A representative of Global Television Network did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
In the story, two men who were deported reported torture, beatings and abuse. One Venezuelan said he was punished with sexual abuse and solitary confinement.
Another was a college student who said guards beat him and knocked out his tooth upon arrival.
“When you get there, you already know you’re in hell. You don’t need anyone to tell you,” he said.
The segment featured numerous experts who called into question the legal basis for deporting migrants so hastily amid pending judicial decisions.
Reporters for the show also corroborated findings by Human Rights Watch suggesting that only eight of the deported men had been sentenced for violent or potentially violent crimes, using available ICE data.

The decision to pull a piece critical of the Trump administration was met with widespread accusations that CBS leadership was shielding the president from unfavourable coverage.
The journalist who reported the story, Sharyn Alfonsi, said in an email sent to fellow “60 Minutes” correspondents that the story was factually correct and had been cleared by CBS lawyers and its standards division.
CBS News chief Bari Weiss said on Monday that the story did not “advance the ball” and pointed out that the Trump administration had refused to comment for the story.
Weiss said she wanted a greater effort made to get its point of view and said she looked forward to airing Alfonsi’s piece “when it’s ready”.
The dispute put one of journalism’s most respected brands – and a frequent target of Trump – back in the spotlight and amplified questions about whether Weiss’ appointment is a signal that CBS News is headed in a more Trump-friendly direction.

data.title: Christmas spirit of compassion can help a Hong Kong that is still healing
data.description:
Christmas this year has come at a time of deep sadness in Hong Kong as the city recovers from the horrific Tai Po fire, remembering those who lost their lives. For many, the joy and merriment that the festive season usually brings will be conspicuous by its absence as efforts continue to help victims of the blaze, which killed at least 161 people and left nearly 5,000 more displaced.
Christmas, falling just ahead of the new year, provides an opportunity for reflection. There will be a steely resolve to ensure lessons are learned and similar tragedies prevented. Action is needed – and is being taken – on many fronts.
This is also a season of compassion and kindness, when thoughts turn to those who are suffering and in need of support. The community, which has come together to assist people affected by the fire, has approached the holiday in that spirit.
Festive events were initially cancelled or postponed as the city mourned. There will be no New Year’s Eve fireworks, but many activities have been repurposed to raise funds for victims of the fire. Operation Santa Claus, the annual campaign organised by the Post and public broadcaster RTHK, has launched an emergency appeal to assist families affected by the tragedy, alongside efforts to draw donations for 13 local charities. Christmas events and activities have gradually resumed, but they are respectful and restrained. This is an appropriate response. There is a balance to be struck.
The colourful lights, uplifting decorations, seasonal markets and friendly gatherings have also returned, from Christmas Town in Central to the forest of festive trees in West Kowloon.
Christmas can be a source of comfort in difficult times. The festival, with its customary good cheer and sense of togetherness, can be heartwarming and help the city come to terms with the tragedy.
The season brings with it a message of peace and goodwill to all, which transcends religion and applies to every part of a world racked by conflict and tension.
In Hong Kong, our thoughts will be with those suffering as a result of the fire. As the city’s healing process begins, Christmas can bring a little light and some hope for the future.

data.title: Will US-led ‘Pax Silica’ shore up minerals supply chains and counter China’s dominance?
data.description:
In its push to secure supplies of critical minerals, the Donald Trump administration has increasingly looked abroad to reduce vulnerabilities in supply chains that underpin many of the world’s most advanced technologies.
From China to the Democratic Republic of Congo to Ukraine, key strands of US foreign policy in recent months have centred on access to these materials – a group of roughly 60 minerals that the US Geological Survey considers vital to national and economic security, including rare earths that have become a focal point of Washington’s trade war with Beijing.
Here, the Post examines a recent initiative pushed out by the second Trump administration to shore up supply chains.
What is the US State Department’s new ‘Pax Silica’ initiative?
The nine-country initiative, launched in December, is aimed at securing AI-related supply chains in areas such as critical minerals, semiconductor design and energy.
Member countries are expected to pursue projects together, including joint ventures and co-investments.


In the words of Jacob Helberg, US Undersecretary of State for Economic Affairs, the initiative aims to build a “vibrant ecosystem of vendors and suppliers” across Pax Silica countries to support resilient supply chains.
Focused on US allies and partners, it also seeks to build what organisers have described as a “new economic security consensus”.
How does it compare to other US critical minerals-oriented initiatives?
The Pax Silica initiative marks a departure from the Biden-era Mineral Security Partnership (MSP), which Helberg described as largely a “buyers’ club” comprising advanced economies such as Europe, Japan and Canada.
“This is fundamentally different,” Helberg said at a briefing. Unlike the MSP, Pax Silica was designed as a supply-side strategy from the outset. Helberg said the initiative prioritises engagement with countries that are home to major silicon producers, with the aim of building direct channels of communication with companies that manufacture and operate key parts of the global supply chain.
“There’s some teeth here,” said Chris Berry, president of House Mountain Partners, an independent metals consultancy based in Washington, noting that while the new initiative shares elements with the MSP – like its focus on like-minded countries with complementary strengths – the context is different.
“The broader Trump administration approach to negotiating agreements and the threat of tariffs … tend to bring people to the negotiating table,” he said.
Berry added that the new initiative reflects the second Trump administration’s emphasis on securing the raw materials that underpin advanced machinery, rather than focusing solely on building or acquiring finished technologies.
Which countries are involved?
The State Department held the inaugural summit with eight other countries in Washington on December 12.
Participating countries included Australia, Britain, Israel, Japan, South Korea, Singapore, the Netherlands and the United Arab Emirates.
Canada, Taiwan, the EU and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) made “guest” contributions.
Six of the eight participating countries – Australia, Britain, Japan, Israel, Singapore and South Korea – signed the summit declaration, which includes a call to address “non-market practices”, a charge Washington frequently levels at Beijing.
Japan and the US signed a separate bilateral joint declaration outlining a shared approach to economic security. Helberg said Pax Silica grew out of bilateral discussions between the two countries.
Each of the chosen countries brings specific strengths, notes Berry, highlighting Japan’s advanced machinery and the Netherlands’ lithography machines, which etch circuits on computer chips.

Which countries are not involved?
India was not invited despite the country’s AI ambitions and its growing importance to both the global technology supply chain and US strategic planning.
Vietnam and New Zealand were other “notable” exclusions, according to Berry.
New Zealand’s absence was surprising given its Five Eyes role but more understandable from a technology and trade perspective, he said.
Vietnam’s exclusion was more puzzling given its status as a top destination for companies seeking to shift production out of China, he continued, raising the possibility that it was being held back as a longer-term bargaining chip.
“My sense with respect to Vietnam and India is that there’s perhaps a larger game being played … to try and counterbalance China’s growth,” he said.


The exclusion of a number of major Indo-Pacific economies reveals the US’ “tiered” alliance-building strategy, other observers say.
Helberg, in a briefing, said that selection was based on countries that focused on semiconductor manufacturing, highlighting Singapore, South Korea, Japan, Taiwan and the Netherlands as the “nucleus” of that effort.
Pax Silica countries are meant for those “who are fundamentally aligned with the United States on broader geopolitical issues and [ones] home to companies that could actually be a meaningful partner in the effort of securing our supply chains”.
He noted that other countries, like Angola, a major mineral exporter, could be included in the future, and that the US would hold bilateral discussions with countries such as Argentina, India, and Taiwan, rejecting the idea that political tensions were behind the exclusions.
What kind of dent might it make in the competition with China?
“If your definition of success is a fully decoupled semiconductor or battery supply chain … I don’t think we will ever see that,” said Berry.
Instead, he argued that progress should be measured by gradual reductions in concentrated dependencies, saying even lowering China’s share of the rare earth magnet market from more than 90 per cent to around 70–75 per cent over several years would represent “a huge win”.
Beijing’s official reaction to Pax Silica was measured, with a Chinese foreign ministry spokesperson urging those involved to “adhere to the principles of a market economy and fair competition”.

Why is there a heightened US focus on critical minerals?
Critical minerals are not just core inputs for clean energy systems and advanced weapons, but also essential to power the fleets of data centres and AI infrastructure that the Trump administration is racing to build.
That shift is expected to drive a sharp rise in demand for key materials. Copper, for example, is set to see explosive growth as Trump pushes to accelerate a nationwide data centre buildout.
“If the 20th century ran on oil and steel, the 21st century runs on compute and the minerals that feed it,” reads a statement from Helberg on the State Department’s website, referring to the hardware resources and processing power necessary to train and run AI models.
Among the most important critical minerals are rare earths, which are used in everything from fighter jets to computer chips and are primarily controlled by China. Beijing’s moves to restrict the flow of rare earths to the US in response to Washington’s trade restrictions temporarily closed several US auto factories. Restoring US access to those materials later emerged as one of the key outcomes of Trump’s trade truce with Chinese President Xi Jinping.

data.title: Meet Samuel Arnold, who plays Julien in Emily in Paris – aka the frenemy of Lily Collins’ character
data.description:
Emily in Paris’ highly anticipated season five premiered on December 18, and fans are eagerly diving into adventures of marketing executive Emily Cooper (Lily Collins) in Paris, and more recently in Rome. And Samuel Arnold is the French actor bringing sharp suits and a sharper side‑eye to the show’s snarky but loyal PR executive Julien.

In season one, we saw Emily and Julien navigate a rivalry that quickly turns into a platonic frenemy situation.
Here’s everything you need to know about Samuel Arnold.
He grew up in Paris and trained for the stage

Born in Paris in 1991, Arnold fell in love with the arts early on, from theatre and drawing to music and film. Per culture publication Euphoria Magazine, the 34-year-old initially trained as a dancer before shifting gears to acting. He attended the École Internationale de Création Audiovisuelle et de Réalisation and the Giles Foreman Centre for Acting in London.
Arnold made his on-screen debut in 2016 in the French TV movie Tuer un Homme and has also acted in theatre productions like Antony & Cleopatra at the National Theatre, which was filmed live and released in 2018.
Emily in Paris was his breakthrough

In an interview about season three with Grazia magazine, Arnold described his life before and after landing Emily in Paris as different as “night and day”. The actor said the role has opened new avenues for him, with “way more opportunities” available.
However, he admitted that the best part about his role is playing a character that couldn’t be more different from himself. “The way he is, this character, and his behaviour, it’s so far from me, so it was a bit of a challenge, but it’s so, so, so enjoyable for an actor to be able to create a character like that,” he explained.
He’s worked behind the scenes

After five years of Emily in Paris, Arnold is also exploring career opportunities behind the camera. He made his executive producer debut with his short film, Nolan’s Treasure, starring himself.
“I would love to act, produce, direct, do everything that I can with my time on Earth. I really view myself as an artist, and I think that storytelling is the best thing an artist can do,” he told Grazia magazine.

data.title: Can Beijing count on Taiwan’s KMT to advance reunification plans?
data.description:
All eyes are on how Beijing’s “Chinese dream” of eventual Taiwan reunification will unfold at a time when the leader of the United States – Taipei’s top international backer – appears less likely to intervene in case of a cross-strait conflict. In the second of a three-part series, Amber Wang looks at the challenges facing the new Kuomintang leader. Read the first part here.
There have been seven chairs of the Kuomintang, Taiwan’s main opposition party, since President Xi Jinping took the top job in Beijing in 2012. But few received a congratulatory message as laden with such high expectations as Cheng Li-wun did when she was elected the party’s new chair in October.
In a rare gesture that went beyond the usual general wording about promoting cross-strait relations, Xi called for a joint effort to promote reunification.
Cheng is expected to visit the Chinese mainland soon, where she is likely to meet Xi following a precedent set by her two predecessors.
Analysts believe Beijing sees a new opportunity in the victory of Cheng, who has voiced a strong Chinese identity and vowed to promote cross-strait reconciliation, in sharp contrast to the stance of Taiwan’s ruling, independence-leaning Democratic Progressive Party (DPP).
In recent months, Beijing has launched a series of initiatives aimed at swaying Taiwanese public opinion and its latest five-year plan reiterated its view that reunification was a national goal.
Yet challenges loom for Cheng, the KMT’s second female leader and an unconventional figure expected to shake up the 100-year-old party. She is already faced with internal resistance, pressure from coming local elections, and the delicate task of navigating ties with both Beijing and Washington.
She has adopted a Beijing-friendly tone, stressing the need to maintain cross-strait peace for “a century”, adding that she was willing to meet Xi to discuss ways to resolve historical differences and build up goodwill.
Her message – and the open expression of a Chinese identity by some of her supporters on the island – has sparked heated discussion on the mainland.

A video of one of Cheng’s supporters, moved to tears after her victory, saying, “I am Chinese and China is our future,” went viral on mainland social media the next day – with more than 30 million views.
“Will reunification come sooner than we thought?” some commenters asked.
Another said they “can’t wait for the day” when they could visit Taiwan.
But some observers have said that while Cheng may be vocal in her opposition to Taiwanese independence, she may not be aligned with Beijing’s reunification goals.
“Compared with the previous letter to former KMT leader Eric Chu [Li-luan], this letter [congratulating her on her victory] reflects Xi’s higher expectations for Cheng regarding cross-strait relations,” said Yang Kai-huang, a professor from the Taiwan-based Ming Chuan University.
“I believe the Communist Party of China is seizing this opportunity.”
Cheng is the first former DPP member to lead the KMT. She was a DPP member of the National Assembly, a now abolished branch of the legislature, between 1996 and 2000, before quitting the party two years later.
She joined the KMT in 2005, serving as a party spokeswoman before being elected to the Legislative Yuan.
Her election comes amid ongoing tensions in the Taiwan Strait, which have intensified since the DPP’s William Lai Ching-te took office as the island’s leader in May last year and clashed repeatedly with Beijing.
Beijing has historically looked to the KMT to boost cross-strait exchanges and, eventually, political consultations between the two sides towards reunification.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
Wang Jianmin, a cross-strait analyst at Minnan Normal University, Fujian province, said Cheng represented a “positive opportunity for Beijing”, citing her opposition to independence and support for the 1992 consensus, an unofficial understanding that there is only one China but the two sides may disagree about what that means.
“This gives both sides a basis for engagement,” Wang added.
Cheng has promised to make her party a stabilising force in Taiwan. “We are not troublemakers, we want peace, not war,” she said last month.
She has also insisted that she would not allow the island to become the next Ukraine.
This message appears to have gone down well in both Washington and Beijing.
Last month, Cheng met Raymond Greene, director of the American Institute in Taiwan, the de facto US embassy, who told her: “The US has never sought conflict across the Taiwan Strait.
“The primary goal is to avoid war and ensure that any cross-strait differences are handled peacefully and without coercion.”


In a recent interview with The New York Times, Cheng said Taiwan must accept its historical status as part of China or risk a devastating war.
“I don’t believe that time is on Taiwan’s side,” she said. “The rapid rise of mainland China means that its national strength is incomparable to what it was just four years ago, let alone 10 years ago.”
Former KMT vice-chairman Andrew Hsia Li-yan also called for a “pragmatic” approach to cross-strait disputes during a visit to Shenzhen earlier this month.
“The future direction is very clear: that national reunification is the path that must be followed,” he told a cultural event in the mainland city.
There seems to be new hope of improved collaboration in the opposition camp as well. The KMT is currently the largest party in the legislature and can command a majority in alliance with the smaller Taiwan People’s Party (TPP).
In November, Cheng called for greater cooperation between the two opposition parties, saying they should coordinate their efforts in local elections to avoid splitting the opposition vote.
The KMT and TPP failed to form a joint ticket for the January 2024 general election, which ultimately helped Lai win – sealing the third straight leadership term for the DPP.
But Sun Yafu, vice-president of the Association for Relations Across the Taiwan Strait, told an event in the mainland province of Hebei last month that cross-strait relations had seen some “positive developments” this year, in part due to “changes in Taiwanese public opinion”.
A survey by Taiwanese paper United Daily News released in late September found that 63 per cent of respondents were dissatisfied with Lai’s handling of cross-strait relations.
However, the KMT’s influence may be waning after three straight leadership election losses, as mainland debate intensifies over the party’s ability to advance Beijing’s reunification agenda.
The mainland will not pin all its hopes on one person. They should understand that peaceful reunification depends on themselves
Yang Kai-huang, professor, Ming Chuan University
In a recent article for the Shanghai-based platform Guancha.cn, Jin Canrong, a professor at Renmin University, argued that some KMT figures – Cheng among them – held positions that diverged significantly from Beijing’s core objectives.
Cheng’s repeated references to “creating a century of cross-strait peace”, he wrote, effectively endorsed long-term separation.
Analysts warned that Beijing needed to have realistic expectations for Cheng and the KMT.
“It is impossible for Cheng to adopt a cross-strait policy fully aligned with the mainland,” Wang said.
“And you cannot expect the KMT to become like the [KMT-breakaway] New Party, which strongly advocates reunification. It is already beneficial if she can help ease tensions.”
Yang echoed that, saying: “The mainland will not pin all its hopes on one person. They should understand that peaceful reunification depends on themselves.”
Just days after she became KMT leader, Cheng faced questions about her authority within the party as several heavyweights skipped her inauguration ceremony.
“Cheng is facing difficulties both inside and outside the party,” Wang said. “As a representative of new generations and a reform-minded figure, she is likely to encounter strong resistance from established KMT factions, as well as from the combative DPP from the outside.”
Beijing and Taipei signed multiple agreements while the KMT’s Ma Ying-jeou was the island’s leader between 2008 and 2016.
But in recent years, Beijing’s focus has shifted more towards engaging the Taiwanese public directly, or “placing our hope in the people of Taiwan”, as Xi described it.
Under this slogan, Beijing has stepped up decades-long efforts to encourage Taiwanese to work, study and settle on the mainland by offering a variety of incentives.
In 2023 it also proposed an “integrated development” zone in Taiwan-facing Fujian province to boost economic ties.


Cheng’s attitude to the US and Japan, traditionally Taiwan’s two main international partners, is also likely to come under intense scrutiny.
She has already been invited to visit both countries, but has criticised Taipei’s plan to raise defence spending to 5 per cent of gross domestic product, something US President Donald Trump had been pushing for.
She told the German TV channel Deutsche Welle that Germany and Nato were “unwilling” to do this, and said Lai had only agreed to do so “under pressure from the US”.
However, she added that the island should determine its own fate and should not be handed over to Beijing or Washington.
Meanwhile, she has been less critical of Japan’s Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi compared with some senior KMT figures such as Ma, who said he did “not welcome” the Japanese leader’s comments about Taiwan.
Takaichi is embroiled in a major row with Beijing after she said a crisis in the Taiwan Strait could be a “survival-threatening” situation for Japan that may justify military intervention.
But in a meeting with Kazuyuki Katayama, head of the Japan-Taiwan Exchange Association’s Taipei office, Cheng said maintaining peace and stability was a shared interest of Tokyo and Taipei – comments that would not be welcomed by Beijing, which regards the matter as an internal one.
Cheng has also avoided directly criticising Takaichi, saying that Japan could only be at peace when there was peace in the Taiwan Strait, but the KMT and Communist Party “must first reconcile”.
Beijing has reacted with a string of retaliatory measures since Takaichi made the comments in early November, including Japan travel warnings, cancellation of cultural exchanges, and restoring a ban on Japanese seafood imports.
Earlier this month, Chinese and Russian nuclear-capable bombers came close to Japanese air space during a joint patrol. But unlike previous statements about similar joint patrols – which usually take place once or twice a year – Beijing did not say that the flights were not aimed at a third party.

data.title: US and China must get serious about AI risk
data.description:
In November 2024, US president Joe Biden and Chinese President Xi Jinping made their first substantive joint statement about the national security risks posed by artificial intelligence (AI). Specifically, they noted that both the United States and China believe in “the need to maintain human control over the decision to use nuclear weapons”.
That may sound like diplomatic low-hanging fruit, since it would be hard to find a reasonable person willing to argue that we should hand control over nuclear weapons to AI. But with the Chinese government, there is no such thing as low-hanging fruit, especially on weighty security matters.
The Chinese are inherently sceptical of US risk reduction proposals. Russia had opposed similar language in multilateral bodies. Because bilateral talks with the US on AI and nuclear security would open daylight between Russia and China, progress on this front was not a foregone conclusion.
In the end, the result was significant because it demonstrated that two AI superpowers can engage in constructive risk management even as they compete vigorously for AI leadership.
Moreover, diplomats and experts from our two countries had also met earlier in 2024, in Geneva, for an extended session dedicated to AI risks. Though it didn’t produce any significant results, the very fact that it occurred was an important step.
Now, as the momentum behind AI development and deployment, both civil and military, gathers pace, the US and China need to build on this foundation by pursuing sustained, senior-level diplomacy on AI risks, even as each strives for the lead in the AI race.


As AI capabilities advance and diffuse, non-state actors could leverage them to threaten both the US and China. Such threats could take many forms, including cyberattacks, novel bioweapons, disinformation campaigns and AI-enabled lethal drones that can strike anywhere.
Nor do the risks stop there. As the US and Chinese militaries increase their use of AI-shortening decision loops, the risk of AI-powered systems inadvertently triggering a conflict or catastrophic escalation will grow. As AI becomes increasingly central to the global banking system, AI-powered trading could cause a market crash.
And looking further ahead, one can imagine a powerful, misaligned AI system threatening grievous harm to humanity. As the world’s only AI superpowers, the US and China need to engage one another directly to address these and other dangers.
Engagement does not mean that China and the US will stop competing. As national security adviser during the Biden administration, I worked hard to ensure that the US maintained its lead on AI so that the technology will work for us rather than against us. I saw that the race for leadership in military, intelligence, commercial applications and adoption of American and Chinese AI models and applications by countries around the world would only continue to heat up.


But it is precisely because of this intense competition that diplomacy is essential. It would be deeply irresponsible for the US and China to race ahead without engaging each other on the risks, or without talking about the immense opportunities that AI presents to address transnational challenges – from the climate crisis to public health.
To be sure, leading thinkers have participated in “Track 2” diplomatic efforts. Such discussions are valuable, and they should continue. Ultimately, though, there is no substitute for direct government-to-government engagement.
Nor can engagement wait, given the breathtaking speed of technological advancement and the foreseeable difficulties of reaching diplomatic breakthroughs. Managing AI risks is uncharted territory, so progress will be neither swift nor easy.
Many commentators have drawn parallels to nuclear arms control and that analogy has some merit. Superpowers bear responsibility for managing the risks associated with powerful technologies, and we have successfully discharged that responsibility in the past through arms control agreements. But AI also presents different challenges, requiring more innovative approaches.


First, verification is more vexing. Counting missiles and warheads, with their detectable signatures, is one thing; counting algorithms is quite another.
Second, the challenge of dual use presents itself differently. There is a fairly straightforward line between peaceful nuclear power and nuclear weapons. By contrast, the same AI model that can help advance scientific research and generate growth can also help deliver lethal effects.
Third, arms control discussions have focused chiefly on states threatening other states. AI risk involves state-to-state threats but also non-state threats and the risks associated with AI misalignment. This presents different challenges and opportunities for diplomacy.
Fourth, at least in the US, AI development is driven not by the government but by the private sector – and not by one company, but by many competing against each other. That means a wider range of actors must be involved in discussions.
Lastly, there is a wide spectrum of views on how fast and how far AI capabilities will go. Some see it as a “normal” technology whose full adoption will take decades, while others argue a superintelligence explosion is just a few years away. With nuclear weapons, you basically knew what you were dealing with. The evolution of AI capabilities is much less clear.
The nuclear arms control framework did not emerge overnight. It took years to devise the relevant controls, protocols and guard rails – and it took decades of diplomacy to maintain them. With AI, we are at the opening stages of something similar in ambition, but different in substance and complexity. That makes it all the more important to proceed with risk reduction efforts immediately.
Copyright: Project Syndicate
